prompt: What does the context window measure, and what does it include?
choices: The maximum number of separate API calls allowed per minute
The maximum tokens the model can consider at once, covering both input and output
Only the number of input tokens you are allowed to send
The model's permanent long-term memory of all past refactors
explanation: The context window is the total number of tokens the model can attend to at once, and it covers both the input you send and the output it generates. It is not just input, and it isn't permanent memory or a rate limit.

prompt: During a long multi-file refactor, why must the AI tool re-supply the prior context on every call?
choices: Because re-sending makes the model respond faster
Because each API call is stateless — the model retains no memory between calls
Because the provider deletes old messages after one minute
It doesn't — the model automatically remembers all earlier files and edits
explanation: Each API call is stateless — the model keeps no memory between calls, so the tool must resend the prior context every time for the model to 'see' it. Re-sending isn't about speed, and the model does not automatically remember earlier files.

prompt: Which of the following are valid strategies for staying within the context window while working across a large codebase or a long requirements thread? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Summarizing an earlier interview or transcript into a short recap
Switching to a faster model
Retrieving only the few files relevant to the current edit
Keeping only the most recent N turns (sliding window)
explanation: Summarizing, retrieving only relevant files, and keeping a sliding window of recent turns all reduce how many tokens you must fit in the window. A faster model doesn't change how many tokens your content needs, so it doesn't help here.

prompt: An AI assistant doing a 30-file refactor starts ignoring a 'keep the old column name' rule it was given at the start. Which fact best explains the root cause to the team?
choices: Retrieval always corrupts the edit history
The model's training finished, so it stopped learning
As the transcript grew past the window limit, the early rule was dropped and is no longer seen by the model
The model was swapped for a smaller version
explanation: As the transcript grew past the window limit, the earliest content — including the rule stated at the start — fell out of context, so the model can no longer see it. Model size and training are unrelated to this dropout.

prompt: You need a model to work across a large codebase where exact details matter and files cross-reference each other. Summarize-then-work vs retrieve-relevant-chunks — which *trade-off* fits?
choices: Paste the entire codebase every call so nothing is ever missed
Retrieve the specific relevant chunks per question — a summary would lose the exact details you need
Summarize the whole codebase once and work from the summary — it's the most compact
Switch to a smaller, faster model so it concentrates better
explanation: When exact details matter and files cross-reference each other, retrieving the specific relevant chunks preserves precision, whereas a single summary would blur the exact details you need. Pasting the whole codebase every call usually won't fit, and model size has no effect on the window's capacity.
